mappings.rationaleAI: The Spanish Empire is the central subject of outline division B, covering discovery and conquest of the Caribbean and Americas (B.1), colonial administration via the Council of the Indies (B.2.a), Indian policy including encomienda (B.2.b), colonial economy and silver mining (B.2.c), Bourbon administrative reforms (B.4), and colonial expansion into North America (B.5).

Columbus's four voyages (1492–1504) inaugurate outline B.1 (Spanish discovery, exploration, and conquest of the Caribbean islands, Mexico, Central America, Peru, Venezuela, Colombia, and Rio de la Plata, 1492–c. 1550), making him the foundational figure for the section's colonial narrative.

North America as a continental overview covers the geography and ethnography of the Americas (A), Norse and English explorations (C.1–C.2), the 13 Colonies (C.3), and French settlements including New France (D), addressing several of the uncovered priority paths.

The Age of Discovery covers Portuguese and Spanish transatlantic voyages initiating the colonial era (B.1), northern European exploration of the Americas relevant to C.2 and D, and the Columbian Exchange that underlies the section's colonial transformation narrative.

The introduction of Black slaves is explicitly cited in B.2.b (Spanish Indian policy) and B.3.a (Portuguese Brazil's introduction of black slavery); the article covers the triangular trade, Caribbean and South American plantation labor, and the role of English, French, and Dutch powers relevant to B.6.

South America covers the geography and ethnography of the continent (A), Spanish conquest and colonial rule (B.1), and Portuguese colonization of Brazil (B.3), addressing the priority path A (geography and ethnography of the Americas) and the core colonial content.

The British Empire's origins in Caribbean and North American colonies directly corresponds to outline C.3 (development of the English colonies), including the founding of the 13 Colonies, economic policies including Navigation Acts, and colonial administration, as well as B.6 (English territorial expansion into areas of Spanish colonization).

Mexico's pre-Columbian civilizations, Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire, three centuries of colonial rule, and settlement patterns connect directly to B.1 (Spanish conquest of Mexico), B.2 (Spain's colonial empire), and B.5 (Spanish colonial expansion into North America).

The American Revolution ended the section's colonial period for the 13 Colonies; it covers the political grievances (Navigation Acts, taxation), colonial self-government, and the War of Independence that culminated the C.3.b narrative on economic and political colonial development.

The United States as a country encompasses the pre-revolutionary history of the 13 Colonies (C.3), their economic, political, and social development (C.3.b), and cultural achievements (C.3.c), covering the colonial period to c. 1790 that forms the section's scope.

Colombia covers pre-Columbian settlement and Spanish colonization from 1499, directly relevant to B.1 (Spanish conquest of Venezuela, Colombia, and other territories) and B.2 (Spain's colonial empire administration in South America).

Washington's leadership of the Continental Army and presidency connects to C.3.b.ii (colonial administration and self-government), though his primary historical significance belongs to section 965; as a figure within the colonial period, he represents the transition described in C.3.b.

The Viking Age covers Norse voyages to Greenland and North America (c. 1000 AD) that directly correspond to outline C.1 (Norse voyages to Greenland and North America, c. 1000), one of the uncovered priority paths adjacent to D.

Mesoamerica covers the historical cultural region's pre-Columbian civilizations (Olmec, Maya, Aztec), addressing the geography and ethnography of the Americas (priority path A) and the context for Spanish conquest beginning in B.1.

The Aztec Empire and Spanish conquest of 1521 directly correspond to B.1 (Spanish conquest of Mexico, c. 1492–1550) and contribute to the ethnography of the Americas (A); the article covers tribute systems, Nahuatl culture, and Hernán Cortés's conquest.

The Inca Empire (c. 1438–1572) covering the western Andes and its Spanish conquest beginning 1532 corresponds to B.1 (Spanish conquest of Peru, 1492–c. 1550) and the pre-Columbian ethnography of the Americas (A).

Canada's history of French and British colonization, Indigenous peoples, New France, and the fur trade directly addresses the French colonial content in priority paths D and D.1, as well as English colonial settlement in C.3.

Colonialism as a theoretical and historical concept encompasses the entire section's subject matter: Spanish and Portuguese colonization (B), English colonial settlement (C), and French colonization (D), with particular relevance to the encomienda system (B.2.b) and mercantilism (B.2.c) cited in the outline.

France's colonial empire encompassing New France and Louisiana corresponds directly to priority paths D (French discoveries and settlements in North America) and D.1 (New France: missionaries, Indian relations, fur trade), as well as B.6 (French territorial expansion into Spanish/Portuguese areas).

Argentina (Rio de la Plata) is explicitly named in B.1 among the territories of Spanish discovery, exploration, and conquest (1492–c. 1550), and the colonial economy and administration (B.2) applied to the region.

Brazil is the direct subject of outline B.3 (exploration and colonization by the Portuguese from 1500), with B.3.a covering colonial economic policies including black slavery, gold mining, and agricultural development, and B.3.b the establishment of captaincies and centralized royal control.

The Enlightenment's influence in the colonies corresponds to C.3.c (colonial cultural and scientific achievements: American Philosophical Society, newspapers, public education, Great Awakening), representing the intellectual life of the English North American colonies in the section's period.

The Rocky Mountains' coverage of North American geography, Indigenous peoples' history, and European exploration connects to the geography and ethnography of the Americas (priority path A), which has no VSI recommendations, though the article's primary focus is post-1790 westward expansion.

The Protestant Reformation is a background cause for colonial settlement in C.3.a (religious reasons for the founding of the 13 Colonies), as Puritan and other Protestant groups fled religious persecution in England to settle New England.

Spain's history of colonial empire in the Americas—from Catholic Monarchs through Bourbon reforms—maps to the entire B division, including discovery/conquest (B.1), colonial administration (B.2), Bourbon decentralization (B.4), and North American settlements (B.5).

Locke's natural rights theory and government by consent directly influenced colonial political thought and the ideological foundations of the 13 Colonies' self-governance (C.3.a, C.3.b.ii), as the Carolina colony's fundamental constitutions were partly co-authored by him.

Elizabeth I's reign (1558–1603) encompasses the early English exploration and attempted settlement referenced in C.2 (1497–c. 1600), including Raleigh's attempts at Roanoke Colony and the maritime exploration of Drake and others.

Imperialism as a theoretical framework encompasses the section's entire subject of European colonial expansion in the Americas, covering mercantile capitalism, chartered companies, and coercive colonial institutions described across B, C, and D.

New York City (founded as Dutch Fort Amsterdam c. 1624, later taken by the English) is directly relevant to C.2 (Dutch settlement and later expulsion by the English) and C.3 (development of the English colonies in North America), being one of the most significant colonial-era cities in the section's coverage.

Human history as a broad survey covers the pre-Columbian civilizations and European colonization era that forms the backdrop of the section, addressing the geography and ethnography of the Americas (priority path A) through its coverage of early civilizations and exploration.
